original_hypothesis.title: distributeTaxTokens Bypasses Intended Autoswap Economics
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The distributeTaxTokens function allows anyone to bypass the autoswap mechanism, sending raw agent tokens directly to the tax recipient instead of converting them to WETH/assetToken.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The protocol relies on _autoSwap to convert accumulated agent token taxes into WETH/assetToken for the project treasury. However, distributeTaxTokens is external and lacks access control. An attacker can continuously call this function whenever projectTaxPendingSwap > 0, forcing the treasury to accumulate illiquid agent tokens instead of the intended base asset.

proof_of_signal.pattern: distributeTaxTokens is external with no access control modifiers

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fallback distribution mechanism is exposed publicly rather than restricted to admin/owner.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time tax tokens are accumulating before the autoswap threshold is reached

operational_questions: Does the project treasury rely on receiving base assets (WETH) for operations?
Can the treasury easily liquidate raw agent tokens without crashing the price?
validation_requirements: Generate tax volume below autoswap threshold
Verify raw tokens are sent to recipient and pending swap is zeroed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor contract for accumulating tax tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call distributeTaxTokens before the autoswap threshold is reached
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tax tokens are sent directly to recipient
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat continuously to completely starve the treasury of base asset revenue

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Treasury revenue model is disrupted; forced to hold illiquid tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - admin can manually swap tokens, but requires constant operational overhead
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Protocol revenue generation and fee distribution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent calls to distributeTaxTokens from unknown addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Treasury receiving raw tokens instead of WETH
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Automated bot calling the function on every tax accumulation

validation_result.mechanism: missing access control on tax distribution
validation_result.impact_summary: Anyone can call distributeTaxTokens to force raw agent tokens to the tax recipient instead of the intended autoswap-converted pairToken, completely disrupting the treasury revenue model.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The distributeTaxTokens function at AgentToken.sol is external with no access control modifier. It allows any caller to zero out projectTaxPendingSwap and send the corresponding agent tokens directly to projectTaxRecipient. This bypasses the _autoSwap mechanism, which is designed to convert accumulated tax tokens to pairToken (WETH/assetToken) via Uniswap before sending to the recipient. An attacker can continuously call this function whenever projectTaxPendingSwap > 0 — even tiny amounts below the autoswap threshold — ensuring the autoswap never triggers. Every other state-changing function affecting tax configuration (setProjectTaxRecipient, setSwapThresholdBasisPoints, setProjectTaxRates, withdrawETH, withdrawERC20) is protected by onlyOwnerOrFactory. The absence of any access control on distributeTaxTokens is a missing check defect.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is confirmed: distributeTaxTokens at AgentToken.sol lines 509-514 is external with zero access control modifiers. The function reads projectTaxPendingSwap, zeros it, and calls _transfer(address(this), projectTaxRecipient, projectDistribution, false). The applyTax=false parameter ensures no further tax is applied. This contrasts with _swapTax which performs a Uniswap swap of agent tokens to pairToken before sending proceeds to projectTaxRecipient. The code is defective because it lacks the onlyOwnerOrFactory modifier that every other privileged tax-related function uses.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control modifier (onlyOwnerOrFactory or similar) on a function that fundamentally alters how tax revenue is distributed. Anyone can call this to send raw agent tokens instead of allowing the autoswap to convert them to pairToken.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker monitors projectTaxPendingSwap (public state variable) for any accumulation > 0
2. Attacker calls distributeTaxTokens() — no access control check
6. _transfer moves raw agent tokens from contract balance to projectTaxRecipient
7. The autoswap threshold is never reached because projectTaxPendingSwap is continuously zeroed
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The normal tax flow is: buy/sell triggers _taxProcessing (AgentToken.sol ~line 399-425) which accumulates tax in _balances[address(this)] and projectTaxPendingSwap. When threshold is met, _autoSwap (line 430+) calls _swapTax (line 469+) which swaps via Uniswap router to pairToken and sends to projectTaxRecipient. However, distributeTaxTokens (line 509) short-circuits this by directly transferring raw agent tokens and zeroing projectTaxPendingSwap. Since it has no access control, anyone can front-run the autoswap by calling distributeTaxTokens whenever taxes accumulate, ensuring the Uniswap conversion never happens.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that configure distributeTaxTokens access. The function is part of the AgentToken implementation which is deployed via factory clones.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward and requires only basic contract interaction. An attacker (or bot) monitors the projectTaxPendingSwap public variable on any AgentToken instance. Whenever it becomes > 0, the attacker calls distributeTaxTokens(). This zeroes projectTaxPendingSwap and sends raw agent tokens to projectTaxRecipient. The gas cost is minimal (a simple ERC20 internal transfer). The attacker can do this on every block where taxes accumulate, ensuring the autoswap mechanism never triggers. The projectTaxRecipient (treasury/TBA) then holds illiquid agent tokens instead of the intended pairToken (WETH/assetToken). The treasury must then manually sell these tokens, incurring slippage and operational overhead.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM impact. The treasury revenue model is disrupted for any AgentToken with taxes enabled. Instead of receiving automatically-converted pairToken (base asset like WETH), the tax recipient receives raw agent tokens. These tokens may be illiquid and selling them manually would cause price impact. The attack can be sustained indefinitely at minimal gas cost. While the treasury doesn't lose the economic value (they receive equivalent agent tokens), the form is significantly worse — illiquid project tokens vs liquid base assets. Admin can mitigate by manually calling _swapTax equivalent or selling tokens, but this requires constant operational overhead.
recommended_mitigations: Add onlyOwnerOrFactory modifier to distributeTaxTokens to restrict who can trigger the fallback distribution
Alternatively, restrict to onlyOwnerOrFactory or projectTaxRecipient so the intended recipient can choose to receive raw tokens if desired
If the function must remain permissionless (e.g., for recovery purposes), add a timelock or require that autoswap has failed a certain number of times before allowing direct distribution
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AgentToken with non-zero buy/sell tax and a swap threshold of e.g. 50 BP. 2. Add initial liquidity. 3. Execute a buy from the LP to generate some tax (projectTaxPendingSwap > 0 but below threshold). 4. From a random EOA (non-owner, non-factory), call distributeTaxTokens(). 5. Verify projectTaxPendingSwap is now 0. 6. Verify projectTaxRecipient received raw agent tokens (not pairToken). 7. Repeat to show autoswap never triggers.
uncertainties: The developer explicitly documented this as an intentional fallback mechanism, which could indicate the missing access control is a deliberate design choice rather than an oversight — though the inconsistency with all other tax functions being protected suggests it is indeed an oversight
The practical impact depends on the liquidity depth of the agent token and whether the tax recipient has infrastructure to handle raw token sales
additional_testing_needed: Test whether the owner/factory can still trigger autoswap after distributeTaxTokens has zeroed projectTaxPendingSwap but the contract still holds tokens
Measure gas cost of continuous distributeTaxTokens griefing vs autoswap savings for the attacker
Test interaction between distributeTaxTokens and _autoSwap when both trigger in the same block
